content.text: In every production readiness review, some checklist items will be incomplete at the target launch date. A team may not have finished a security penetration test, may not yet have a full monitoring dashboard, or may be waiting on a legal review that depends on a third-party contract. The choice is binary: delay the launch or proceed with documented awareness of what is incomplete. When a team chooses to proceed, the formal mechanism is a PRR exception with accompanying risk acceptance documentation. This sub-subtopic explains what that means, how to structure the documentation, and who holds the authority to accept different categories of risk.
What It Is

A PRR exception is a formal record that a specific production readiness checklist item cannot be completed before the planned launch date, combined with an explicit decision by a qualified authority that the associated risk is acceptable. It is not a workaround, a shortcut, or a way to pretend the gap doesn't exist. It is an honest acknowledgment that a gap exists, paired with a documented plan for what will be done about it and by when.

The distinction between an exception and simply ignoring a checklist item is accountability: the exception creates a record of what was known, who decided to proceed anyway, what compensating controls are in place, and when the gap will be remediated. That record has legal, regulatory, and operational significance. Without it, a post-incident investigation may find that a team knowingly launched with a security gap but left no evidence that the decision was made deliberately at the appropriate level of authority.

PRR exceptions are time-bounded. They are not permanent waivers. Every exception carries an expiry date, a remediation commitment, and a review cadence. An exception that is still open three months after launch without a remediation update has become a liability.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI systems are genuinely complex to make fully production-ready before launch. Evaluation sets are never complete. Bias audits surface findings that take time to remediate. Prompt injection mitigations may be partially implemented when business pressure demands a launch. The question is not whether exceptions will be filed — they will — but whether they will be handled with appropriate rigor.

The risk of an informal exception process is that the decision to proceed with a gap is made implicitly, at the wrong level, without documentation. A developer pushes to production without completing the security review because the team lead said "we'll do it after launch." Nobody wrote that down. Nobody with authority to accept that risk reviewed it. When the vulnerability is later exploited, the organization cannot demonstrate that the decision to proceed was made deliberately, at the right level, with a remediation plan. The result is personal and organizational liability that a formal exception process would have managed.
Benefits
Visibility.
Exceptions make gaps visible to the organization. A register of open exceptions for a system is a clear picture of its technical and compliance debt.
Appropriate authority.
By defining who can accept each risk category, the exception process ensures that decisions with broad consequences are not made unilaterally by individual contributors.
Remediation pressure.
A time-bounded exception with a named owner creates accountability for closing the gap. Without a formal record, post-launch remediation often gets deprioritized indefinitely.
Audit defensibility.
In a regulatory audit or legal proceeding, a documented exception with an appropriate approver is far better than evidence of an undocumented known gap.
Launch enablement.
Well-designed exception processes allow launches to proceed safely when gaps are genuinely low risk or have effective compensating controls, rather than blocking launches until every item is perfect.
Pattern recognition.
A register of exceptions across launches reveals systemic gaps — the evaluation process is always incomplete at launch, suggesting the evaluation timeline is structurally insufficient.
Trade-offs
Exception inflation.

If the exception process is too easy, teams treat it as a permanent escape hatch. Every hard checklist item becomes an exception rather than a completed task. Prevent this with a firm cap on the number of open exceptions any system can carry and escalation requirements for repeat exceptions.
Administrative overhead.

Formal exception documentation takes time. For low-risk gaps with obvious compensating controls, the documentation overhead may exceed the actual risk management value. Consider a lightweight fast-track process for genuinely minor gaps.
Staleness.
An exception that auto-expires but whose remediation is not actually complete creates a false sense of closure. Expiry must trigger a genuine review, not just a timestamp update.
False confidence.
A signed exception can create an illusion that the risk has been managed, when in fact it has only been acknowledged. Compensating controls must be genuinely effective, not performative.
How It Works

The exception lifecycle has four phases: identification, documentation, approval, and closure. Identification happens during the PRR review process when a checklist item is flagged as incomplete. The gap owner (the person responsible for completing the item) drafts the exception document. The document is reviewed by the relevant function head (e.g., the CISO reviews a security exception) and escalated if the risk severity requires a higher authority level. The approved exception is tracked in the exception register with a defined review cadence. Closure happens when the remediation is complete and verified, at which point the exception is marked closed with evidence.
Risk Acceptance vs. Risk Mitigation vs. Risk Transfer

Risk acceptance means an authority deliberately chooses to proceed knowing the risk exists and has decided it is tolerable. Risk mitigation means putting compensating controls in place that reduce the likelihood or impact of the risk materializing. Risk transfer means shifting the financial or operational consequence of the risk to a third party (through insurance, contractual indemnification, or SLAs with external providers). An exception document should clearly state which of these applies — often it is a combination of all three.
Exception Document Structure

A well-structured exception document contains six required fields and two optional fields. Omitting any of the required fields is itself a compliance gap. The structure is consistent across the organization so that reviewers can quickly assess any exception, regardless of which team filed it.
Risk statement.

A concise, specific description of what gap exists and what risk it creates. "Security penetration test not complete" is insufficient. "Prompt injection penetration test covering the agent's tool-call endpoints has not been completed. Risk: a malicious user input could cause the agent to call the delete_record tool outside its intended scope." is adequate.
Risk owner.
The named individual accountable for remediating the gap. This must be a specific person, not a team or a role.
Severity classification.
Low, medium, high, or critical, based on a defined impact and likelihood matrix. Severity determines who has authority to accept the risk.
Mitigation / compensating controls.

What is in place today to reduce the risk while the gap exists. For the prompt injection example: "Tool calls are restricted to an allowlist of approved tool names validated at the API gateway layer, reducing the blast radius if injection occurs."
Remediation plan and timeline.

Specific steps and a committed date for closing the gap. "Pen test will be completed by [date]. Results will be reviewed by the security team. Any critical or high findings will be remediated before the next release."
Expiry date.

The date on which the exception automatically expires and must either be closed (remediated) or explicitly re-approved with a new review. Maximum exception duration should be defined in policy — typically 30 days for critical exceptions, 90 days for high, 6 months for medium.
Acceptance criteria (optional).
What evidence is required to close the exception. "Clean pen test report with no critical or high findings" is a closure criterion; "pen test completed" is not.
Approval record (required).
Name, title, and timestamp of the authority who accepted the risk. This is the document's legal and governance anchor.
Architecture

attrs.alt: PRR Exception Lifecycle: Gap Identified → Risk Statement → Severity Classification → Approver Selection → Exception Document → Approver Decision. Accepted path leads to Launch Proceeds with exception tracking; Rejected path leads to Launch Blocked for remediation.

content.text: The exception lifecycle flows from gap identification through a formal risk statement and severity classification to approver selection. The exception document consolidates all required fields and is submitted to the appropriate approver. The approver's decision either allows the launch to proceed (with the exception tracked and reviewed on a cadence) or blocks it until the gap is remediated.
Who Can Accept Risk: Authority Matrix

Risk acceptance authority must be calibrated to risk severity. The principle is that the authority level required to accept a risk should be proportional to the potential organizational impact if the risk materializes. A low-risk exception accepted by a developer is appropriate. A critical security exception accepted by the same developer is not.
Exception Type
Severity
Minimum Approver
Max Duration
Review Cadence
Monitoring gap (non-critical path)
Low
Engineering lead
90 days
Monthly
Incomplete evaluation set
Medium
ML lead + product director
60 days
Bi-weekly
Prompt injection mitigation pending
High
CISO or VP Engineering
30 days
Weekly
Regulatory compliance gap
Critical
CEO / Board (with legal sign-off)
14 days
Weekly
Bias testing incomplete
Medium–High
Responsible AI lead + CLO
45 days
Bi-weekly
AI/GenAI-Specific Exception Categories

Agentic AI systems have exception categories that are uncommon in conventional software PRRs. Teams new to AI PRRs may not recognize these as formal exception types and may attempt to treat them as routine engineering debt rather than documented risk decisions.
Evaluation Coverage Gaps

The evaluation set (the benchmark used to assess model quality before launch) is never complete. At launch, a team may have covered 80% of the intended test scenarios. The remaining 20% may include edge cases, adversarial inputs, and low-frequency user intents that are hardest to write tests for. An exception documents which coverage areas are incomplete, what the expected accuracy is in uncovered areas based on extrapolation, and when full coverage will be achieved.
Prompt Injection Mitigations Partially Deployed

A full prompt injection defense for an agentic system may include input sanitization, output filtering, tool-call allowlisting, and canary token monitoring. If two of four layers are in place at launch, an exception documents which layers are missing, what the residual attack surface is, and the implementation timeline for the missing layers. The compensating control description must be specific about what the deployed layers actually prevent.
Bias Testing Incomplete

Bias audits for customer-facing AI systems are time-consuming and may involve external reviewers. If the audit is not complete at launch, the exception must describe which demographic dimensions have been tested, which have not, and what the plan is for completing the audit. It must also describe what user-facing disclosures are in place about the system's known limitations.
Model Drift Monitoring Incomplete

An agentic system that uses a third-party LLM is subject to provider model updates that may change behavior without notice. The ideal state includes automated quality regression testing against every provider model update. If this is not in place at launch, the exception documents the manual process that will be used to detect drift (e.g., weekly manual review of output samples) until automated monitoring is implemented.
When to Use It
Use a PRR exception when a checklist item cannot be completed by the launch date due to timeline constraints, resource unavailability, or external dependencies outside the team's control.
Use it when a risk can be adequately mitigated by compensating controls even though the checklist item is not technically complete.
Do not use it as a substitute for doing the work. An exception for an item that could have been completed with normal prioritization is a process failure.
Do not use it for critical safety items that have no adequate compensating control. Some gaps must block launch; no exception is appropriate.
Never use it for items where the risk is not understood — "we haven't looked at this yet" is not an exception; it is a missing review.
How to Implement

# prr_exception_template.yaml
# Fill in all required fields. Submit to the PRR sign-off owner for your function.

exception_id: EXC-2024-089           # assigned by the PRR tracking system
prr_id: PRR-2024-047
system_name: GenAI Customer Chatbot v2.0
created_by: alice@corp.com
created_at: "2024-11-15T10:00:00Z"

# --- REQUIRED FIELDS ---

risk_statement: |
  The prompt injection penetration test covering the agent's tool-call endpoints
  (specifically the customer_data_query and account_update tools) has not been
  completed. A successful injection could cause the agent to call account_update
  with attacker-controlled parameters, potentially modifying account data outside
  the intended user's own account.

risk_owner: carol@corp.com            # named individual; must not be a team or alias

severity: high                        # low | medium | high | critical
likelihood: medium                    # low | medium | high
impact: high                          # low | medium | high

compensating_controls:
  - description: >-
      Tool calls are validated against a static allowlist of permitted tool names
      at the API gateway before reaching the agent.
    coverage: prevents calls to tools not on the allowlist
  - description: >-
      The account_update tool enforces row-level security: it can only modify
      the account belonging to the authenticated session user.
    coverage: limits blast radius of any successful injection
  - description: >-
      All tool calls are logged with full input/output to a tamper-evident audit
      log. Anomaly detection rules will alert on unusual tool-call patterns.
    coverage: enables rapid detection and response

remediation_plan:
  steps:
    - "Engage external red team for prompt injection pen test by 2024-11-29"
    - "Review findings with security team within 5 business days of report delivery"
    - "Remediate all critical and high findings before next release"
    - "Re-test after remediation to confirm closure"
  committed_date: "2024-12-13"

expiry_date: "2024-12-15"            # exception auto-expires; must be closed or re-approved
review_cadence: weekly               # daily | weekly | bi-weekly | monthly

acceptance_criteria: |
  Exception closes when: external pen test report shows no critical or high
  prompt injection findings against the tool-call endpoints. Report must be
  reviewed and accepted by the CISO.

# --- APPROVAL RECORD (filled by approver) ---
approval:
  approver_name: "Jordan Lee"
  approver_title: "CISO"
  approved_at: "2024-11-16T14:30:00Z"
  approval_comment: |
    Approved with compensating controls in place. Pen test must be completed
    and all critical findings remediated before December 15. If findings are
    critical and cannot be remediated by that date, the exception must be
    escalated to VP Engineering for a go/no-go decision on continued launch.

# --- CLOSURE RECORD (filled when remediated) ---
closed_at: null
closed_by: null
closure_evidence: null
Tracking Exceptions to Closure

# exception_tracker.py — track open PRR exceptions and surface overdue items
# pip install pyyaml rich

import yaml
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from rich.table import Table
from rich.console import Console

def load_exceptions(exceptions_dir: Path) -> list[dict]:
    """Load all exception YAML files from a directory."""
    exceptions = []
    for path in sorted(exceptions_dir.glob("EXC-*.yaml")):
        data = yaml.safe_load(path.read_text())
        exceptions.append(data)
    return exceptions

def days_until_expiry(exception: dict) -> int:
    expiry = datetime.fromisoformat(exception["expiry_date"]).replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    return (expiry - now).days

def report_open_exceptions(exceptions: list[dict]) -> None:
    console = Console()
    table = Table(title="Open PRR Exceptions")
    table.add_column("ID", style="cyan", width=14)
    table.add_column("System", width=22)
    table.add_column("Severity", width=10)
    table.add_column("Owner", width=18)
    table.add_column("Expires", width=12)
    table.add_column("Days Left", width=10)
    table.add_column("Status")

    for exc in exceptions:
        if exc.get("closed_at") is not None:
            continue  # skip closed exceptions

        days_left = days_until_expiry(exc)
        sev = exc["severity"]
        sev_color = {"critical": "red", "high": "yellow",
                     "medium": "blue", "low": "green"}[sev]

        if days_left < 0:
            status = "[bold red]EXPIRED — remediate or re-approve[/bold red]"
        elif days_left <= 7:
            status = f"[yellow]⚠ Expiring soon ({days_left}d)[/yellow]"
        else:
            status = f"[green]Active ({days_left}d)[/green]"

        table.add_row(
            exc["exception_id"],
            exc["system_name"][:22],
            f"[{sev_color}]{sev}[/{sev_color}]",
            exc["risk_owner"].split("@")[0],
            exc["expiry_date"][:10],
            str(days_left),
            status,
        )

    console.print(table)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    exceptions_dir = Path(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else Path("exceptions/")
    if not exceptions_dir.exists():
        print(f"Directory {exceptions_dir} not found")
        sys.exit(1)
    exceptions = load_exceptions(exceptions_dir)
    open_count = sum(1 for e in exceptions if e.get("closed_at") is None)
    print(f"\nTotal exceptions: {len(exceptions)} | Open: {open_count}")
    report_open_exceptions(exceptions)
Mental Model

Think of a PRR exception as a surgical informed consent form. A patient undergoing surgery accepts a set of known risks — infection, anesthetic complications, the possibility that the surgery does not achieve its intended outcome. Those risks are documented, explained, and explicitly accepted by the patient before the procedure begins. The surgeon does not proceed without the signature. The signature does not make the risks go away, but it creates an accountable, documented record that the decision was made deliberately by someone with authority to make it.

A PRR exception is the organization's informed consent to launch with a known risk. The risk statement is the disclosure. The compensating controls are the surgical safeguards. The remediation plan is the follow-up care. The expiry date is the follow-up appointment. The approver's signature is the patient's consent. Without the documentation, the procedure proceeds without informed consent — which is both ethically and legally unacceptable.
Common Mistakes
Open-Ended Exceptions with No Expiry

An exception without an expiry date is a permanent waiver. The gap never gets closed because no one is held accountable for remediating it. Every exception must have an expiry date, and that date must be enforced — expired exceptions without closure evidence must trigger an escalation, not a silent extension.
Wrong Approver Level

A high-severity security exception approved by an individual contributor has no governance value. If the risk materializes, the organization cannot claim that appropriate authority reviewed and accepted the risk. The authority matrix must be enforced, not treated as a suggestion.
Vague Risk Statements

"Security review incomplete" is not a risk statement. A risk statement must describe what specific gap exists, what specific harm could result, and what the likelihood and impact are. A vague risk statement means the approver cannot make an informed decision about whether to accept the risk.
Compensating Controls That Are Not Controls

"We will monitor the situation" is not a compensating control — it is a plan to notice if something goes wrong, not a mechanism to prevent it. Compensating controls must actively reduce the likelihood or impact of the risk, not merely provide visibility into failures.
Best Practices
Maintain a centralized exception register.
All open exceptions across all systems should be visible in one place, organized by system, severity, and expiry date.
Automate expiry notifications.
The risk owner and their manager should receive automated reminders at 14 days, 7 days, and 1 day before expiry. Expired exceptions should trigger an immediate escalation.
Set caps on concurrent open exceptions.
A system with more than five open exceptions should be flagged for a leadership review of whether its overall risk posture is acceptable.
Require closure evidence, not just closure timestamps.
When an exception is marked closed, the closure must reference specific evidence — a test report, a completed audit, a merged pull request — not just a date.
Review the exception pattern in launch retrospectives.

If the same exception category appears in multiple launches (e.g., evaluation coverage is always incomplete at launch), the problem is structural and should be addressed at the process level, not just accepted launch by launch.
Never extend an exception without re-approval.
Extending the expiry date is a new risk acceptance decision that requires the same approver authority as the original acceptance.
Performance and Scalability
For organizations running frequent AI deployments, the exception process must be designed to handle volume without becoming a bottleneck. A few structural optimizations help:
Pre-approved exception templates for common low-risk gaps.

If the same type of low-risk gap appears frequently (e.g., monitoring dashboard for a non-critical metric not complete at launch), create a pre-approved template that any engineering lead can apply without triggering the full exception review workflow.
Batch exception reviews for standing review panels.
A weekly security exception review meeting where the CISO reviews all pending high-severity exceptions is more efficient than scheduling one-off reviews for each exception.
Automation for low-severity exception approval.

Low-severity exceptions with a completed risk statement and remediation plan can be auto-approved (with notification to the function lead) if they meet defined criteria, reducing manual review overhead.
Tooling and Ecosystem
JIRA.

Exception tickets linked to the parent PRR epic, with custom fields for severity, expiry date, risk owner, and approval record. A JIRA filter showing all open exceptions ordered by expiry date is a practical exception register.
Confluence.
Exception pages created from a template within the PRR Confluence space. A macro that aggregates all open exceptions across all PRR pages into a dashboard view.
Notion databases.
A centralized exceptions database with properties for each required field and views filtered by status, severity, and system. Notion automation can notify owners on expiry approach.
YAML/JSON in version control.
Storing exception documents as YAML files in the repository alongside the system's PRR documents gives them version history and enables automated processing by scripts like the tracker shown above.
ServiceNow Risk module.
Enterprise risk management platforms provide structured exception tracking with workflow automation, escalation routing, and audit-trail preservation out of the box.
Security and Reliability

Exception documents are compliance artifacts and must be protected accordingly. They contain descriptions of known vulnerabilities and gaps that could be used by an attacker if disclosed. Access to the exception register should be restricted to people with a need to know — typically PRR owners, function heads, and compliance personnel — not broadcast across the organization.
Protect exception records from unauthorized access.
An exception describing a prompt injection vulnerability in production is sensitive information. Store exception documents with appropriate access controls.
Retain exception records for the appropriate period.
Even closed exceptions should be retained for the duration required by your regulatory framework — they are part of the audit trail demonstrating that risks were managed appropriately.
Treat a pattern of expired unclosed exceptions as a security signal.

If exceptions regularly expire without being remediated, the organization has known, persistent vulnerabilities that are not being closed. This is a risk management failure that needs executive attention.
Key Takeaways
A PRR exception is not a workaround — it is a formal, documented, authority-approved decision to proceed with a known gap, paired with a time-bounded remediation commitment.

Every exception must contain a specific risk statement, named risk owner, severity classification, genuine compensating controls, a remediation plan with a committed date, an expiry date, and a named approver with sufficient authority.
Authority to accept risk must be calibrated to severity: critical exceptions require executive or board-level approval; low-severity exceptions may be approved by engineering leads.

AI-specific exception categories — evaluation coverage gaps, incomplete prompt injection mitigations, bias testing not complete, and model drift monitoring absent — must be recognized as formal exception types, not informal engineering debt.
Open-ended exceptions with no expiry are effectively permanent waivers; every exception must have an enforced expiry date that triggers a genuine review or escalation when reached.
An exception register that surfaces patterns across launches is a valuable diagnostic tool for identifying structural gaps in the PRR process that need to be addressed systemically.
Exception documents are sensitive compliance artifacts — restrict access appropriately and retain them for the duration required by your regulatory framework.
